items.myth: `src/` becomes part of the import path — users will write `import src.ragapp`.
items.reality: `src/` is a *source-tree* concept. The build backend strips it; the wheel ships `ragapp/`, and users write `import ragapp` like always.
items.example: Inspect the wheel: `unzip -l dist/ragapp-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl` — no `src/` prefix anywhere.

items.myth: `src/` layout is needed only for libraries you publish to PyPI.
items.reality: It pays off in any project with tests or CI. The isolation between source and metadata is the win, not the publishing.
items.example: An internal-only data pipeline benefits as much: tests in CI match what runs in the deployed container.

items.myth: `src/` layout makes editable installs slower or less responsive.
items.reality: Editable installs (`pip install -e .`) work identically — your edits to files in `src/ragapp/` are visible immediately.
items.example: Edit `src/ragapp/chunker.py`, then in a Python REPL: `from ragapp.chunker import chunk_text; chunk_text(...)` reflects the edit (after `importlib.reload(...)` or new process).

items.myth: Flat layout is fine because 'pytest can find my package'.
items.reality: Pytest finds it via `rootdir`/`sys.path` heuristics — convenient but unprincipled. Tests pass in ways your users' installs can't reproduce.
items.example: A flat-layout test using `from helpers import x` works because `tests/` happens to be on `sys.path`; the same import won't work post-install.

items.reality: It usually *fixes* hidden CI problems. The change is mechanical: move package, add `where = ['src']`, install in CI step.
items.example: Replace `pytest` in CI with `pip install -e . && pytest`. Done.
